coredata.dc:title: Foliar application of nano carbon dots increases grain yield in rice via enhancing leaf photosynthesis and stem assimilates translocation under nitrogen deficiency

Ni, Chen
Zhu, Changjin
Hu, Qiuqian
Zhang, Yan
Xu, Jiwei
Zhou, Cheng
Xu, Ke
coredata.dc:description:
Context Rice is one of the world's most important food crops, and the excessive use of fertilizers to achieve high rice yields has led to global environmental concerns. Therefore, it is critical to reduce nitrogen fertilizer input, a problem for which nano carbon dots (NCD) provide a promising solution. However, few studies have examined the effect of NCD on rice yield formation under reduced nitrogen input conditions.
Objectives The aim of this study is to evaluate the effects of NCD on the photosynthetic characteristics, accumulation and translocation of stem assimilates, and yield of rice under reduced nitrogen conditions.
Methods A two-year field experiment was conducted on rice using two varieties, Yongyou2640 and Y liangyou2, with five treatments: N1 (270 kg N ha−1), N2 (216 kg N ha−1), N3 (162 kg N ha−1), CN2 (216 kg N ha−1+foliar application of NCD), and CN3 (162 kg N ha−1+ foliar application of NCD). Leaf photosynthetic enzyme activity, stem and grain starch synthesis-related enzyme activity, sucrose translocation-related enzyme and gene expression, and yield and yield components were measured at five time points from the jointing stage to maturity.
Results Under the condition of reducing nitrogen fertilizer by 40 %, the foliar application of NCD could significantly increase rice yield, with average increases of 13.2 % and 12.3 % for both varieties in 2022 and 2023, respectively, reaching the yield achieved with normal nitrogen levels. The foliar application of NCD significantly increased the leaf photosynthetic rate by increasing rubisco enzyme activity, resulting in a 8.5–61.5 % increase in the photosynthetic rate for both varieties at different growth stages across the two years. In addition, the foliar application of NCD prior to heading increased the stem activity of ADP-glucose pyrophosphorylase (AGP) and starch synthase (StS), thereby increasing the accumulation of NSC in the stems before flowering. The average increases for both varieties were 21.2 % and 17.8 % in 2022 and 2023, respectively. After heading, the activities of α-amylase, β-amylase, and sucrose phosphate synthase (SPS) in the stems, as well as the activities of AGP, StS, and cell wall invertase in the grains, were significantly increased. The expression of key genes involved in sucrose phloem loading in the stem, OsSUTs and OsSWEETs, and genes related to sucrose phloem unloading in the grains, OsSUTs, OsSWEETs, and OsCINs, were upregulated, thereby promoting the translocation of assimilates from the stems to the grains, which improved the grain filling percentage and consequently increased the grain yield under conditions of nitrogen deficiency.
Conclusion In summary, under conditions of nitrogen deficiency, the foliar application of NCD to rice can increase the activities of photosynthetic enzymes, starch metabolism enzymes in stems and grains, and sucrose translocation-related enzymes; increase sucrose translocation-related gene expression; regulate the accumulation and translocation of assimilates; and improve grain filling and grain yield, and also increase economic profit.

head.ce:title: Foliar application of nano carbon dots increases grain yield in rice via enhancing leaf photosynthesis and stem assimilates translocation under nitrogen deficiency

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rice is one of the world's most important food crops, and the excessive use of fertilizers to achieve high rice yields has led to global environmental concerns. Therefore, it is critical to reduce nitrogen fertilizer input, a problem for which nano carbon dots (NCD) provide a promising solution. However, few studies have examined the effect of NCD on rice yield formation under reduced nitrogen input conditions.
The aim of this study is to evaluate the effects of NCD on the photosynthetic characteristics, accumulation and translocation of stem assimilates, and yield of rice under reduced nitrogen conditions.

A two-year field experiment was conducted on rice using two varieties, Yongyou2640 and Y liangyou2, with five treatments: N1 (270 kg N ha−1), N2 (216 kg N ha−1), N3 (162 kg N ha−1), CN2 (216 kg N ha−1+foliar application of NCD), and CN3 (162 kg N ha−1+ foliar application of NCD). Leaf photosynthetic enzyme activity, stem and grain starch synthesis-related enzyme activity, sucrose translocation-related enzyme and gene expression, and yield and yield components were measured at five time points from the jointing stage to maturity.

Under the condition of reducing nitrogen fertilizer by 40 %, the foliar application of NCD could significantly increase rice yield, with average increases of 13.2 % and 12.3 % for both varieties in 2022 and 2023, respectively, reaching the yield achieved with normal nitrogen levels. The foliar application of NCD significantly increased the leaf photosynthetic rate by increasing rubisco enzyme activity, resulting in a 8.5–61.5 % increase in the photosynthetic rate for both varieties at different growth stages across the two years. In addition, the foliar application of NCD prior to heading increased the stem activity of ADP-glucose pyrophosphorylase (AGP) and starch synthase (StS), thereby increasing the accumulation of NSC in the stems before flowering. The average increases for both varieties were 21.2 % and 17.8 % in 2022 and 2023, respectively. After heading, the activities of α-amylase, β-amylase, and sucrose phosphate synthase (SPS) in the stems, as well as the activities of AGP, StS, and cell wall invertase in the grains, were significantly increased. The expression of key genes involved in sucrose phloem loading in the stem, OsSUTs and OsSWEETs, and genes related to sucrose phloem unloading in the grains, OsSUTs, OsSWEETs, and OsCINs, were upregulated, thereby promoting the translocation of assimilates from the stems to the grains, which improved the grain filling percentage and consequently increased the grain yield under conditions of nitrogen deficiency.

In summary, under conditions of nitrogen deficiency, the foliar application of NCD to rice can increase the activities of photosynthetic enzymes, starch metabolism enzymes in stems and grains, and sucrose translocation-related enzymes; increase sucrose translocation-related gene expression; regulate the accumulation and translocation of assimilates; and improve grain filling and grain yield, and also increase economic profit.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rice (Oryza sativa L.) is one of the most important food crops in China, with an annual output of about 210 million tons, accounting for 28 % of the world's rice supply (Deng et al., 2019). Improving rice yield plays an important role in ensuring food security in China and globally. Nitrogen is one of the decisive factors affecting its photosynthesis and yield of rice, promoting the formation of the canopy required for photosynthesis, affecting grain filling, and ultimately driving yield (Chen et al., 2022). However, large amounts of nitrogen fertilizer application leads to serious problems, such as low nitrogen use efficiency, serious air and water pollution, soil acidification, ozone depletion, increased greenhouse gas emissions, biodiversity loss, climate change, and decreased rice quality (Cui et al., 2018; Cai et al., 2023; Waqas et al., 2023). On the other hand, the photosynthesis of leaves and the translocation of NSCs stored in stems are sources of assimilates for yield formation (Yoshida, 1972), excessive nitrogen fertilizer input can cause chlorophyll content saturation in leaves, resulting in the interception of most of the light radiation by the upper leaves of the canopy while the lower leaves experience poor light conditions, which consequently reduces the photosynthetic efficiency of the canopy (Zhou et al., 2023); high amounts of nitrogen result in low translocation rates of non-structural carbohydrates (NSCs) to the grains of rice, which results in poor grain filling and severe yield losses (Yang and Zhang, 2018). In addition, high nitrogen concentrations also result in eventual grain yield loss due to lodging because of nitrogen’s strong ability to promote plant height, especially in rice (Hu et al., 2023). Therefore, facing an increasing demand for food, serious challenges remain in terms of reducing the impact of excessive nitrogen input on the environment while maintaining high rice grain yields.

Nitrogen fertilizer input can be reduced through nutrient, agronomic, crop, and soil optimization management strategies, which require comprehensive consideration of regional differences (Cai et al., 2023; You et al., 2023). Improper nitrogen management measures leading to nitrogen deficiency may significantly reduce rice leaf area, chlorophyll content, and rubisco content and activity, resulting in a decrease in the photosynthetic rate and the production of photosynthetic assimilates (Xiong et al., 2016). Meanwhile, reducing the nitrogen input can improve stem assimilate translocation and promote grain filling by regulating the activity of starch–sucrose metabolizing enzymes and the protein and gene expression levels of sucrose transporters (Li et al., 2018a, 2022b). Therefore, strategies for the synergistic improvement of photosynthetic assimilate production and translocation are urgently needed to promote grain filling and maintain a stable or increased rice yield with reduced nitrogen input.

Such a strategy may be “nano agriculture,” which was recently developed (Li et al., 2023). Nano carbon dots (NCD) are a new type of carbon-based nanoparticle with good biocompatibility and water solubility which are non-toxic to plants, do not pollute the environment, and can be made from abundant low-cost sources (Li et al., 2020). They can promote crop growth and development and increase crop photosynthesis and yield (Li et al., 2018b; Wu et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2020). A series of studies have proposed possible mechanisms for NCD to improve crop growth and yield due to the following effects: (1) NCD promote root and plant growth, which may be related to the hormone analogues produced by the degradation and transformation of NCD within cells; (2) NCD improve photosynthesis and the carbohydrate content by enhancing the activity of photosynthesis-related enzymes in both rice and corn, which may be important; (3) NCD promote grain filling and yield by upregulating the expression of sucrose transporter genes in maize, which may enhance the sucrose translocation capacity of the phloem, thereby increasing the translocation of photosynthetic assimilates to the grain (Li et al., 2018b; Wang et al., 2021). Given the important roles of nitrogen and NCD in maintaining high leaf photosynthetic rates and assimilate translocation, a deeper understanding of how NCD affect photosynthesis and stem assimilate accumulation and translocation in rice under reduced nitrogen input conditions is of great significance.

The foliar application of NCD has practical significance in increasing grain yield (Li et al., 2023). In this study, the photosynthesis, sucrose–starch metabolism related enzyme activities, and expression of sucrose translocation-related genes in the leaves, stems, and grains of rice were investigated after the foliar application of NCD at different stages. Therefore, the purpose of this study is to (1) explore the effects of the foliar application of NCD on photosynthesis, the accumulation and translocation of stem assimilates, and grain yield in rice under reduced nitrogen input and (2) combine the physiological and molecular processes of sucrose phloem loading and unloading and sucrose–starch metabolism to elucidate the mechanism of the effects of the foliar application of NCD on grain yield formation under conditions of reduced nitrogen input.

The two-year field experiment was conducted from 2022 to 2023 at the Zhang hongcheng Innovation Experimental Base (119˚32′E, 32˚18′N) of Yangzhou University, located in Chenxing Village, Shatou Town, Yangzhou City, Jiangsu Province, China. The experimental site was located in a subtropical warm monsoon climate zone. Meteorological data for the two years could be found in Fig. 1. The experimental site had sandy loam soil. The soil physiochemical properties in the 0–20 cm tillage layer were as follows: pH, 8.07; organic matter, 32.37 g kg−1; total nitrogen (N), 2.84 g kg−1; available phosphorus (P), 14.80 mg kg−1; and available potassium (K), 122.60 mg kg−1.

The rice varieties Yongyou2640 (YY2640) and Y liangyou2 (YLY2) were used in this experiment, with five treatments applied. These five treatments comprised different nitrogen (N) levels and foliar applications of NCD under different N levels: (1) N1, 270 kg N ha−1; (2) N2, 216 kg N ha−1; (3) N3, 162 kg N ha−1; (4) CN2, 216 kg N ha−1 + foliar application of NCD; and (5) CN3, 162 kg N ha−1 + foliar application of NCD. The experiment adopted a split-plot design, with three replicates for each treatment. The main plots were five treatments and sub-plots were rice varieties. Each plot covered an area of 15 m. At the jointing stage, heading stage, and 20 days after heading (20DAH), an NCD aqueous solution (0.5 mg mL−1) was uniformly sprayed onto the leaves at 40 mL m−2 using a manual sprayer. In both 2022 and 2023, the seedlings were raised in trays with a sowing date of May 19 and transplanted in the paddy field at a seedling age of 24 days on June 12, using a hill spacing of 30 cm × 12 cm with four seedlings per hill. N fertilizer was applied in a 4:2:4 ratio of basal fertilizer, tillering fertilizer, and panicle fertilizer at 1 day before transplanting, 7 days after transplanting and at the jointing stage (approximately 40 days after transplanting). The phosphate fertilizer used was superphosphate, and the potassium fertilizer was potassium chloride, with application rates of 120 kg P2O5 ha−1 and 120 kg K2O ha−1, respectively. Manual weeding was conducted, and insects and diseases were controlled via several applications of chemicals from planting to harvest to avoid yield losses. During the growth period, irrigation was performed continuously and repeatedly using the dry-and-wet approach until 1 week before harvest.

The NCD used in this study were provided by Pro. Zhenhui Kang and Dr. Jian Huang of College of Nano Science & Technology of Soochow University. NCD are a type of carbon-based nanomaterial with fluorescence properties. NCD are synthesized directly from a carbon rod through a mild, one-step electrochemical approach at room temperature. A nano carbon dot has the following material science properties: a hexagonal graphite structure, typical size of 5 nm; surface functional groups of -OH/-COOH, etc.; and an 8–10 % oxygen content (Ming et al., 2012; Li et al., 2018b, 2019). NCD have low toxicity levels, can be manufactured from abundant low-cost sources, and demonstrate excellent biocompatibility. Many researchers have explored the potential applications of CDs in agriculture, and their physiological functions includes accelerating seed germination, enlarging root elongation, improving the photosynthetic rate, enhancing grain production, and increasing plant resistance to disease (Li et al., 2023).

After 3, 30, 40, 50, and 60 days of NCD foliar application at the jointing stage, photosynthetic parameters were measured between 0900 and 1530 h using a LI-6400XT portable photosynthesis system (Li-Cor, Lincoln, NE, USA) with a fluorescence leaf chamber (Li-6400–40; Li-Cor). In the leaf chamber, the leaf temperature was set to 30 °C, the photosynthetic photon flux density was maintained at 1500 μmol m−2 s−1, the CO2 reference was controlled at 400 μmol mol−1, and the flow rate was set to 500 μmol s−1. Photosynthetic parameters were measured at 3 days after jointing using newly and fully expanded leaves from three plants from each treatment group. At 30 days after jointing, both varieties were heading, and measurements at 30, 40, 50 and 60 days after jointing were conducted on flag leaves. All measurements were centered on the midpoint of the leaf. After equilibration to a steady state, the photosynthetic parameters, including the photosynthetic rate and intercellular CO2 concentration, were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dry matter and leaf area index
ce:section.ce:para: At jointing, heading (HD) (80 % of plants headed), and maturity (MT), six plants were sampled and divided into stems, leaves, and panicles (at HD and MT). Leaf area was measured using an LI- 3100 Area Meter (LI- COR, United States), and the leaf area index (LAI, m2 m−2, surface area of leaf per unit ground) was calculated. All parts of the plant were placed in an oven and heated at 105°C for 30 min, and the shoot dry weight was determined after oven-drying at 80°C to a constant weight. The accumulation of dry weight from jointing to heading and from heading to maturity was measured.

Dry samples of stems sampled at HD and MT were ground into powder to determine NSC concentrations using the anthraquinone colorimetric method (Li et al., 2022b). Briefly, 0.1 g of powder sample was extracted with 80 % ethanol in an 80 °C water bath for 30 min. The supernatant was transferred to a 100 mL volumetric flask after centrifugation, and the process was repeated three times. The supernatant was used to determine the concentrations of soluble sugars. After this, 2 mL of distilled water was added to the residue, which was then incubated in a 100 °C water bath for 15 min. After cooling, the pellet was extracted using 9.2 mol L−1 perchloric acid (HClO4) and 4.6 mol L−1 HClO4. The supernatants obtained via two rounds of centrifugation were combined in the same volumetric flask, and the volume was brought to 100 mL via the addition of distilled water. This mixture was used to determine the starch concentration. The concentrations of soluble sugars and starch were measured at 620 nm on a microplate reader (NanoQuant, infinite M200, Tecan, Switzerland), using a colorimetric method with the anthrone reagent as previously described (Li et al., 2022b). The concentration of NSC (mg g−1 dry weight) was the sum of the soluble sugar and starch concentrations. The traits related to NSC translocation in the stems were calculated. The following formulas were used: NSC content (g m−2) = dry weight × NSC concentration; apparent transferred mass (ATM, g m−2) = NSC content at heading - NSC content at maturity; apparent transferred rate (AR, %) = ATM/NSC content at heading × 100; apparent contribution rate (AC, %) = ATM/grain yield × 100.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activities of rubisco and starch–sucrose metabolism-related enzymes
ce:section.ce:para: After the foliar application of NCD at different growth stages, several plants with consistent and uniform growth were chosen from each plot. Fresh samples of their leaves, stems, and grains were collected, rapidly frozen using liquid nitrogen, and then stored at −80°C. The leaf samples were analyzed for their rubisco activity after 3, 30, 40, 50, and 60 days of NCD application at jointing; the stems were assessed for the activity of ADP-glucose pyrophosphorylase (AGP) and starch synthase (StS) after 3, 10, and 20 days of NCD application at jointing and the activity of α-amylase, β-amylase and sucrose phosphate synthase (SPS) after 3, 10, and 20 days of NCD application at heading; and the grain samples were assessed for the activity of cell wall invertase (CWI), AGP, and StS after 3, 10, and 20 days of NCD application at heading. Enzyme activity measurements were carried out by Shanghai Enzyme-linked Biotechnology Co., Ltd (China). UV spectrophotometry was used to determine the activity of these enzymes using reagent kit, according to the instructions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Expression level of sugar translocation-related genes
ce:section.ce:para: At 10 days after heading, sugar translocation-related genes were measured, including the relative expression levels of the OsSUTs (OsSUT1, OsSUT2, and OsSUT4) and OsSWEETs (OsSWEET4, OsSWEET11, and OsSWEET14) in stems and the relative expression levels of the OsSUTs (OsSUT1, OsSUT2, and OsSUT4), OsSWEETs (OsSWEET4, OsSWEET11, and OsSWEET14), and OsCINs (OsCIN1, OsCIN2, OsCIN4, and OsCIN7) in grains. Total RNA was extracted using a Trizol Total RNA Extraction Kit (Sangon Biotech, China), according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Next, the extract was checked for purity via electrophoretic detection, and the concentration was determined using a NanoDrop 2000 micro-spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The first strand of cDNA was then synthesized using Maxima Reverse Transcriptase. Quantitative real-time PCR (qRT-PCR) was performed using the SYBR Green qPCR Master Mix (Roche, Switzerland). The qRT-PCR reaction was carried out using a LightCycler480 II Realtime PCR system (Roche, Rotkreuz, Switzerland). The gene-specific primers for RT-PCR are shown in Table S1. The relative quantification of gene expression was calculated using the 2−ΔΔCT method (Livak and Schmittgen, 2001); each sample included three biological replicates and three technical replicates. The gene expression measurements were completed by Sangon Biotechnology (Shanghai) Co., Ltd (China).

At maturity, 50 hills were surveyed for effective panicles, and six plants were taken from each treatment according to the average number of effective panicles to determine the number of spikelets, grain filling percentage, and grain weight. The samples were separated into leaves, stems, and panicles. The panicles were hand-threshed, and the filled spikelets were then separated from the unfilled spikelets by submerging them in tap water. All filled and unfilled spikelets were air-dried, weighed, and counted. The dry weights of the leaves, stems, rachis, and filled and unfilled spikelets were determined after oven-drying at 80°C to a constant weight. The spikelets per panicle, grain filling percentage (100 × filled spikelets/total spikelets), and 1000-grain weight (g) were calculated. Grain yield was determined from a harvest area of 5 m−2in the center of each plot and adjusted to 14 % moisture.

The total revenue is the value of the rice grains (total revenue = rice purchase price × yield). The production costs includes nitrogen fertilizer, NCD, and other costs, such as phosphorus and potassium fertilizers, pesticides, machinery, seeds and labor. The rice purchase price in Jiangsu Province in 2022 and 2023 was 18.1 USD 50 kg−1 for YY2640, and 17.8 USD 50 kg−1 for YLY2, respectively. The price of common urea in 2022 and 2023 was 446 and 299 USD t−1. The NCD cost was 0.1 USD L−1. Other costs in 2022 and 2023 were as follows: potassium chloride, 118 and 85 USD ha−1; calcium superphosphate, 161 and 131 USD ha−1; machinery, 201 and 199 USD ha−1; pesticide, 62 and 62 USD ha−1; labor cost, 420 and 420 USD ha−1; YY2640 seeds, 331 and 331 USD ha−1; YLY2 seeds, 310 and 310 USD ha−1. The net profit = total revenue − nitrogen fertilizer costs − NCD costs − other costs.

Analysis of variance was conducted using the Statistix 9 software (Analytical software, USA), and the least significant difference (LSD) method was used to compare the significance of the differences in the mean values at the P ≤ 0.05 level. Figures were prepared using the SigmaPlot 10.0 software (SPSS Inc, USA).

As nitrogen levels decreased, the yields of the two rice varieties declined. However, the yield following N2 treatment did not decrease significantly compared to after N1, while the yield following N3 significantly decreased compared to N1 and N2. Compared to N1 and N2, the yield decrease under N3. The yield reductions in 2022 were 17.5 % and 16.3 % for YY2640 and 17.7 % and 12.3 % for YLY2, and in 2023, they were 28.8 % and 25.4 % for YY2640 and 10.9 % and 11.7 % for YLY2. Foliar spraying with NCD did not increase the yield of plants that received CN2 compared to N2, however, foliar spraying with NCD significantly increased the yield following CN3 treatment compared to N3, except there was not significant difference for YLY2 in 2023. The yields of YY2640 and YLY2 increased by 12.4 % and 13.9 % and 15.3 % and 9.2 % in 2022 and 2023, respectively, with no significant difference compared to N1 and N2, except there was significant difference for YY2640 in 2023. Under the CN3 treatment, foliar spraying with NCD reduced the yield losses of YY2640 and YLY2 by 41.9 % and 35.2 % in 2022 and 62.0 % and 24.7 % in 2023, respectively ( Fig. 2).

From the perspective of yield components, as nitrogen levels decreased, the panicle number and spikelets per panicle significantly decreased in both rice varieties, except there was no significant difference in spikelets per panicle for YY2640 between N2 and N3, and for YLY2 between N2 and N3. While the grain filling percentage increased and the thousand-grain weight remained unchanged. Whether at the N2 or N3 level, the foliar application of NCD did not affect the panicle number, spikelets per panicle and 1000-grain weight in either variety. However, the foliar application of NCD increase the grain filling percentage of plants treated with CN2 compared to N2, while the difference was not significant, but it did significantly increase the grain filling percentage of plants treated with CN3 compared to N3, the grain filling percentage of YY2640 and YLY2 increased by 5.5 and 8.5 percentage points in 2022, respectively, and by 6.6 and 5.5 percentage points in 2023, respectively ( Table 1).

As the nitrogen level decreased, the dry matter at the jointing stage, heading, and maturity decreased. The dry matter of plants treated with N3 was significantly lower than that of plants treated with N1 and N2. Between N1 and N2, there was no significant difference except for the jointing stage in 2022. The foliar spraying of NCD had no effect on the dry matter of CN2 plants compared to N2 plants, but it significantly increased the dry matter of CN3 plants compared to N3 plants at heading and maturity, except there was no significant difference for YLY2 at maturity in 2022 and YY2640 at maturity in 2023. Compared with N3, following CN3 treatment, the dry matter of YY2640 and YLY2 at heading increased by 11.2 % and 16.3 % in 2022, respectively, and by 12.4 % and 15.6 % 2023, respectively; at maturity, these increases were 7.6 % and 7.0 %, 7.3 %, and 6.4 %, respectively. Compared with N3, the dry matter accumulation under CN3 from jointing to heading increased by 19.2 % for YY2640 and 23.9 % for YLY2 in 2022 and by 18.5 % for YY2640 and 22.7 % for YLY2 in 2023 without affecting the dry matter accumulation from heading to maturity ( Table 2).

There was no difference in the leaf area index of the two varieties between the N1 and N2 treatments at different stages in 2022 and 2023, although the leaf area index following the N3 treatment was significantly lower than that observed in the N1 and N2 plants, except there was no significant difference for YY2640 at heading in 2023. The foliar application of NCD did not increase the leaf area index following the CN2 and CN3 treatments when compared to N2 and N3, respectively ( Table 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photosynthetic rate and intercellular CO2 concentration
ce:section.ce:para: As growth continued, the photosynthetic rates of the two rice varieties showed a downward trend. With the decrease in the nitrogen level, the photosynthetic rate also showed a downward trend. The foliar application of NCD did not have a significant effect on the photosynthetic rate of CN2 plants compared to N2 plants, but it remarkably increased the photosynthetic rate of CN3 plants compared to N3 plants, and significant differences were observed at 3 days after jointing (DAJ) for YY2640, 30 and 50DAJ for YLY2 in 2022, and at 3 and 30DAJ for YY2640, 3, 40, 50 and 60DAJ for YLY2 in 2023. The increases at different stages were 8.5 %-19.9 % for YY2640 and 10.0 %-17.8 % for YLY2 in 2022 and 18.8 %-59.7 % for YY2640 and 11.2 %-61.5 % for YLY2 in 2023 ( Fig. 3).

At different measurement periods, the intercellular CO2 concentration in the rice leaves showed an increasing trend with the nitrogen level decreasing. The foliar application of NCD reduced the intercellular CO2 concentration in plants treated with CN2 and CN3 compared to N2 and N3, respectively; the impact on CN3 plants was greater than on CN2 plants. The intercellular CO2 concentration in CN3 plants was significantly lower than in N3 plants, except there was no significant difference for YY2640 at 30DAJ in 2022, at 3 and 40 DAJ in 2023. At different periods, the reduction in the intercellular CO2 concentration was 3.7 %-16.7 % for YY2640 and 6.5 %-19.9 % for YLY2 in 2022 and 5.4 %-7.8 % for YY2640 and 5.7 %-8.9 % for YLY2 in 2023 ( Fig. 4).

Different nitrogen levels had no significant effect on the accumulation of stem NSC at heading (except for YLY2 in 2023, when N2 was significantly higher than N1). The foliar application of NCD increased the accumulation of stem NSC at heading, and the impact on CN3 plants was greater than the impact on CN2 plants, and significant difference was observed for YLY2 in 2022. Compared with N3, the CN3 treatment increased stem NSC accumulation by 18.6 % for YY2640 and 23.7 % for YLY2 in 2022, 12.7 % for YY2640 and 22.8 % for YLY2 in 2023 ( Fig. 5).

The foliar application of NCD increased the ATM, AR, and AC. Compared with N2, under the CN2 treatment, the ATM increased by 12.4 % for YY2640 and 33.3 % for YLY2 in 2022 and by 13.9 % for YY2640 and 15.4 % for YLY2 in 2023, and significant difference was observed in YLY2 in 2022; the AR increased by 1.7 for YY2640 and 13.2 for YLY2 in 2022 and by 4.0 for YY2640 and by 12.5 percentage points for YLY2 in 2023, and significant difference was observed in YLY2 in both 2022 and 2023; the AC increased by 1.1 for YY2640 and 1.9 for YLY2 in 2022 and by 2.8 for YY2640 and 1.0 percentage points for YLY2 in 2023, and the differences were not significant. Compared with N3, under the CN3 treatment, the ATM increased by 24.1 % for YY2640 and 52.4 % for YLY2 in 2022 and by 19.1 % for YY2640 and 39.8 % for YLY2 in 2023, and the differences were significant except YY2640 in 2022; the AR increased by 3.5 for YY2640 and 16.1 for YY2640 in 2022 and by 4.0 for YY2640 and 10.9 percentage points for YLY2 in 2023, and significant difference was observed in YLY2 in both 2022 and 2023; while the AC increased by 1.1 for YY2640 and 3.7 for YLY2 in 2022 and by 1.0 for YY2640 and 2.1percentage points for YY2640 in 2023, and significant difference was observed in YLY2 in 2022 ( Fig. 6).

As the growth period progressed, the rubisco activity in the leaves of both varieties showed an increasing trend, followed by a decreasing trend. And as nitrogen levels decreased, rubisco activity showed a decreasing trend. The foliar application of NCD had no significant effect on rubisco activity following CN2 compared to N2, but remarkably increased rubisco activity in CN3 plants compared to N3 plants, and significant differences were observed at 3 and 40DAJ for YY2640, and 14, 30 and 40DAJ for YLY2. During the measurement period, compared with N3, the rubisco activity under CN3 increased by 10.4 %-12.4 % for YY2640 and 9.7 %-13.1 % for YY2640 ( Fig. 7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activity of AGP and StS in stems
ce:section.ce:para: As growth continued, the activities of AGP and StS showed an increasing trend followed by a decreasing trend, and a decrease in nitrogen application reduced the activities of AGP and StS. The foliar application of NCD increased the activity of AGP and StS. The AGP and StS activities of the stems of the two varieties showed no significant difference between the CN2 and N2 treatments, except there was significant difference for YY2640 at 10DAJ, while they were significantly higher in plants that received the CN3 treatment than the N3 treatment, except there were no significant difference for AGP at 3DAJ and StS at 10DAJ in YY2640, and StS at 10DAJ in YLY2. During the measurement period, compared with N3, the AGP activityunder CN3 increased by 7.5 %-21.7 % for YY2640 and 10.7 % and 18.7 % for YLY2; the activity of StS increased by 7.9 %-12.8 % for YY2640 and 8.7 % and 9.8 % for YLY2 ( Fig. 8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activity of α-amylase, β-amylase, and SPS in stems
ce:section.ce:para: As the growth process progressed, the activities of these three enzymes increased, and as nitrogen levels decreased, the activities of these three enzymes increased. The foliar application of NCD increased the activities of α-amylase, β-amylase, and SPS, with no significant difference in the activities of these three enzymes between the CN2 and N2 plants, except there were significant difference for α-amylase at 20 days after heading (DAH) in YY2640 and YLY2, while plants that received CN3 showed significantly higher activity than those that received N3, except there were significant difference for α-amylase at 10DAH in YY2640 and YLY2, for β-amylase at 10DAH in YLY2, and for SPS at 3 and 20 DAH in YLY2. During the measurement period, compared with N3, the α-amylase activities under CN3 increased by 6.5 %-13.8 % for YY2640 and 7.2 %-9.5 % for YLY2; the activity of β-amylase increased by 9.4 %-11.9 % for YY2640 and 8.1 %-11.3 % for YLY2; SPS activity increased by 11.6 %-22.9 % for YY2640 and 12.0 %-18.2 % for YLY2 ( Fig. 9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activity of CWI, AGP, and StS in grains
ce:section.ce:para: As the growth process progressed, the activities of CWI and StS enzymes showed a decreasing trend, while AGP showed an increasing and then decreasing trend. As the nitrogen level decreased, the activities of CWI and StS enzymes increased, while AGP activity decreased. The foliar application of NCD increased the activities of CWI, AGP, and StS, with no significant difference in the activities of these three enzymes between the plants treated with CN2 and N2, except there was significant difference for StS at 3DAH in YLY2, while they were remarkably higher in plants treated with CN3 than N3, and significant differences were observed at 3 and 10DAH for CWI in YY2640 and at 10DAH in YLY2, at 10DAH for AGP in YY2640 and at 20DAH in YLY2, at 3, 10 and 20DAH for StS in YY2640 and at 20DAH in YLY2. During the measurement period, compared with N3, the CWI activities under CN3 increased by 10.7 %-18.2 % for YY2640 and 8.3 %-8.8 % for YLY2; AGP activity increased by 9.5 %-10.7 % for YY2640 and 10.6 %-11.8 % for YLY2; and the activity of StS increased by 11.5 %-14.3 % for YY2640 and 4.9 %-13.8 % for YLY2 ( Fig. 10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Expression of OsSUTs and OsSWEETs in stems
ce:section.ce:para: The expression levels of each gene showed an increasing trend with decreasing nitrogen levels. The foliar application of NCD increased the relative expression levels of OsSUTs and OsSWEETs genes in stems. Compared with N2, under CN2 treatment, the expression level of OsSUTs increased by 27.4 %-54.9 % for YY2640 and 56.4 %-244.6 % for YLY2, and the differences were significant except for OsSUT4 in YY2640 and the expression level of OsSWEETs increased by 36.6 %-127.4 % for YY2640 and 107.9 %-262.5 % for YLY2, and the differences were significant. Compared with N3, under CN3 treatment, the expression level of OsSUTs increased by 29.5 %-68.6 % for YY2640 and 40.3 %-109.7 % for YLY2, and the differences were significant except for OsSUT4 in YY2640, and the expression level of OsSWEETs increased by 32.8 %-85.0 % for YY2640 and 37.3 %-254.2 % for YLY2, and the differences were significant except for OsSWEET11 in both YY2640 and YLY2 ( Fig. 11).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Expression of OsSUTs, OsSWEETs and OsCINs in grains
ce:section.ce:para: The expression levels of each gene increased with decreasing nitrogen levels. The foliar application of NCD increased the relative expression levels of OsSUT, OsSWEET, and OsCIN genes in grains. Compared with N2, under the CN2 treatment, the expression level of OsSUTs increased by 65.9 %-157.0 % for YY2640 and 44.9 %-95.5 % for YLY2, and the differences were significant except for OsSUT2 in YLY2 and OsSUT4 in YY2640; the expression level of OsSWEETs increased by 12.7 %-101.8 % for YY2640 and 48.7 %-143.2 % for YLY2, and the differences were significant except for OsSWEET11 and OsSWEET14 in YY2640; and the expression level of OsCINs increased by 100.5 %-21.7 % for YY2640 and 6.1 %-74.7 % for YLY2, and the differences were significant except for OsCINs in YLY2. Compared with N3, under the CN3 treatment, the expression level of OsSUTs increased by 74.2 %-105.3 % for YY2640 and 103.3 %-152.1 % for YLY2, and the differences were significant; the expression level of OsSWEETs increased by 23.1 %-97.8 % for YY2640 and 17.5 %-105.3 % for YLY2, and the differences were significant except for OsSWEET11 in YLY2 and YY2640, and OsSWEET14 in YY2640; and the expression level of OsCINs increased by 37.9 %-224.8 % for YY2640 and 43.7 %-205.4 % for YLY2, and the differences were observed for OsCIN1, OsCIN4 in YY2640 and OsCIN7 in YLY2 ( Fig. 12).

For Yongyou 2640, N1 treatment achieved the highest yield in both 2022 and 2023, resulting in the highest total revenue of 4036 and 4056 USD ha−1, respectively, followed by N2, CN2, CN3, and N3. The costs of nitrogen fertilizer are consistent between CN2 and N2, with a decrease of 20.2 % and 19.7 % compared to N1 in 2022 and 2023, respectively. And the costs of nitrogen fertilizer of CN3 is consistent with N3, with a decrease of 39.9 % compared to N1 in 2022 and 2023. Due to the additional application of NCD, CN2 and CN3 have an increase of 41 USD ha−1 in NCD cost compared to N1, N2, and N3. The other costs of each treatment are the same, and the net profit trend of each treatment is consistent with the total revenue Compared with N2, the net profit of CN2 slightly decreased (4.8 % in 2022 and 5.3 % in 2023), but compared with N3, the net profit of CN3 increased by 19.7 % in 2022 and 25.8 % in 2023, respectively ( Table 4). For YLY2, the results are basically consistent with YY2640, except that in 2022, CN2 had a net profit of 4.2 % higher than N2, and N2 treatment achieved the highest net profit in 2023. However, compared with N3, CN3 has a increased net profit by 26.0 % in 2022 and 12.8 % in 2023, respectively ( Table 5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of NCD enhanced grain yield of rice under nitrogen deficiency
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the foliar application of NCD on yield was different under different nitrogen reduction levels. Compared with N3, the CN3 treatment significantly increased yield as the nitrogen application of N3 decreased by 50 % compared to N1, for the indica rice variety (YLY2), the yield reached the levels achieved with the N1 and N2 treatments (Fig. 2). Nitrogen deficiency can lead to a decrease in Rubisco enzyme activity, accelerate chloroplast degradation, reduce light energy capture capacity, decrease stomatal conductance, and limit the entry of CO₂ into leaves (Xiong et al., 2016), resulting in a decrease in leaf photosynthetic rate (Fig. 3) and a significant decrease in yield (Fig. 2). And the foliar application of NCD could significantly increase Rubisco activity (Fig. 7), increase the leaf photosynthetic rate (Fig. 3), and significantly increase yield (Fig. 2), which was consistent with previous research results (Li et al., 2018b, 2019). Under N2 conditions, there was no significant change in yield following the foliar application of NCD. This might be because although the nitrogen application rate of N2 decreased by 25 % compared to N1, the soil background nitrogen (2.84 g kg−1) was higher under the conditions of this study (higher than the average of 0.32–1.25 g kg−1 in the region) (Chen et al., 2023), and the nitrogen supply satisfied the growth needs of the rice. This was confirmed by the photosynthesis and yield performance of the two varieties under N2 compared to N1 (Fig. 2, Fig. 3). Therefore, the effect of the foliar application of NCD was not significant, nor did it affect leaf photosynthesis (Fig. 3); therefore, it had no effect on yield or a reduced increased (YLY2 increased by 3.5 % in 2022) (Fig. 2), and its underlying mechanism still needs further clarification. Comparing the two varieties, there was no significant difference in yield between CN2 and N2 in 2022 and 2023, while the CN3 treatment resulted in significantly higher yield than N3. Yields of YY26 and YLY2 increased by 12.4 % and 13.9 %, respectively in 2022, and by 15.4 % and 9.2 %, respectively in 2023. Differences in the impact of NCD on varieties mainly occurred between years. In 2023, regardless of the foliar application of NCD or other nitrogen treatments, the yield of YLY2 was significantly higher than in 2022, while the difference between YY26 and other years was not significant. Possible reasons could include climate factors. Temperatures were high in 2022, and the differentiating stage of pollen mother cells and the meiosis stage occurred 10–15 days before heading in rice, exposured to high temperatures at this time would significantly reduce spikelet fertility and affect the seed setting rate (Yoshida, 1981; Endo et al., 2009). In 2022, YLY2 (heading at August 29) experienced extremely high temperatures during the differentiation of pollen mother cells and meiosis stages (August 14–19), resulting in a significantly lower seed setting rate than in 2023 (decreased by 7.6–11.4 percentage points), while YY2640 (heading at August 10) was less affected, with a seed setting rate only 2.3–4.2 percentage points lower than in 2023 (Table 1). From the perspective of yield components, the impact of NCD on yield was mainly to increase the grain filling (Table 1). Grain filling is related to the supply of assimilates and the activity of enzymes involved in grain starch synthesis; the NCD treatment increased NSC accumulation in the stem before flowering (Fig. 5) and AGP and StS enzyme activities in grains, observations that are consistent with changes in yield (Fig. 2). At different nitrogen levels, the effect of the NCD was different, which is also reflected in the NSC accumulation and AGP and StS enzyme activity. The underlying reasons for these changes require further research.

The NCD concentration applied in this study was 0.5 mg mL−1, which was within the concentration range validated in previous studies (Li et al., 2018b). The relationship between rice yield and the concentration of NCD sprayed still needs further exploration. An appropriate NCD concentration can promote crop growth and increase yield, while an NCD concentration that is too high inhibits crop growth (Chen et al., 2016). Our previous study showed that, when the NCD concentration reached 2.5 mg mL−1, it significantly inhibited rice seed germination (data unpublished). Therefore, during the foliar spraying of NCD, it is necessary to control the concentration to avoid negative effects on the rice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of NCD enhanced leaf photosynthesis of rice under nitrogen deficiency
ce:section.ce:para: Spraying NCD on leaves could increase the leaf photosynthetic rate (Fig. 3), which is consistent with previous studies on rice (Li et al., 2018b). The foliar application of NCD did not alter the photosynthetic area of leaves (Table 3) as it affects the leaf photosynthetic rate by regulating rubisco enzyme activity (Wang et al., 2018). In this study, the rubisco enzyme activity (Fig. 7) and photosynthetic rate were increased in leaves treated with foliar spraying of NCD under CN3 (Fig. 3). The decrease in intercellular carbon dioxide concentration under the CN3 treatment also indicated an enhanced utilization of carbon dioxide by the photosynthetic system, which assimilated more carbon dioxide (Fig. 4). NCD have unique light absorption properties and are excellent electron donors and acceptors (Cao et al., 2011; Mitra et al., 2011). By assisting in the absorption of photons to transfer electrons to chloroplasts, they can accelerate the full electron transfer chain pathway in light reactions, thereby enhancing photosynthesis (Chandra et al., 2014), which may promote the photosynthetic rate. We observed that the application of NCD at the N2 level did not significantly increase the photosynthetic rate of the leaves; nor did it lead to an increase in yield. This result suggests that there may be an interaction between NCD and nitrogen, which may have complex effects on photosynthesis and yield. This mechanism requires further study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of NCD enhanced enzymatic activities related to starch metabolism and expression of genes related to sucrose translocation in stems and grains under nitrogen deficiency
ce:section.ce:para: The foliar spraying of NCD enhanced the activity of starch metabolism and sucrose-transport related enzymes in rice stems and grains (Figs. 8–10). AGP and StS are key enzymes involved in starch synthesis (Nakamura et al., 1989; Chen et al., 2024), and an increase in their activity means that more sucrose is converted to ADPG, accelerating starch synthesis. Before heading, the activities of AGP and StS in the stems increased (Fig. 8), promoting the accumulation of NSC (mainly starch and soluble sugars) in the stems (Pan et al., 2011), which is beneficial for enhancing sink activity (Fu et al., 2011). The foliar application of NCD also increased the levels of activity of α-amylase, β-amylase, and SPS enzymes in the stems during grain filling (Fig. 9), as well as the levels of activity of AGP and StS enzymes in the grains (Fig. 10). High levels of activity of α-amylase, β-amylase, and SPS enzymes promoted the activation and redistribution of stem NSC (Fig. 6) (Yang et al., 2001; Li et al., 2018a), while the increase in AGP and StS activity in the grains accelerated the rate of utilization of sucrose transported to the grains, thereby positively reinforcing the translocation of stem NSC. There are few reports on the effects of NCD on starch metabolism- and sucrose translocation-related enzyme activity. Considering that the surface functional groups of NCD can bind with enzymes to enhance their activity (Gao et al., 2023), NCD may participate in the process of binding enzymes and substrates through surface functional groups, thereby affecting enzyme activity.

The foliar application of NCD significantly increased the expression of OsSUT and OsSWEET genes in stems (Fig. 11) and grains under CN3 (Fig. 12), which encode key proteins involved in sucrose loading at the source (stems) and unloading in the sink (grains) (Patrick et al., 2013; Li et al., 2022a, 2022b). After heading, the sucrose synthesized via the reactivation of NSC in the stems is loaded into the vascular bundle sieve element and transported to the grains by SUT and SWEET. It is then unloaded into the grain by SUT, CWI, and SWEET to further synthesize starch. High levels of expression of sucrose transport-related genes means that more NSC is transported from the stems to the grains (Fig. 6), thereby promoting grain filling (Li et al., 2022b). There have been numerous reports on the use of NCD in crops, and application of NCD increases enzyme activity and gene expression, such as increasing Rubisco activity, the expression of the thionin gene and the gene expression of enzymes related to chlorophyll (Li et al., 2018b, 2021a, 2021b). However, the mechanisms remain unclear. Carbon dots, as a type of photoluminescence nanomaterial, there are abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface, such as carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl, etc., provide them with characteristics such as good water solubility, adsorption capacity, catalytic activity, and easy functionalization (Li et al., 2020). Therefore, a possible regulatory mechanism for enzyme activity is the stabilization of the enzyme structure, where the abundant functional groups on the surface of carbon dots bind with enzyme molecules to stabilize the natural conformation of the enzyme, possibly via interface effects and substrate enrichment, the adsorption of substrate molecules due to the high specific surface area of carbon dots and surface functionalized sites, increased local concentration, and the promotion of contact between substrates and enzyme active centers. The synergistic catalytic mechanism involves carbon dots possessing enzyme-like activity, such as peroxidase activity (Yang et al., 2024). Li et al. (2018b) reported that NCD could enter the nucleus, loosen DNA structure, and upregulate gene expression. However, the specific mechanism by which NCD enhance genes expression related to the sucrose loading and unloading process still needs further research.

In this study, the foliar spraying of NCD had different effects on the physiological processes and yield of rice under different nitrogen levels. Although the foliar spraying of NCD could increase stem- and grain-related enzyme activity and gene expression levels following CN2 treatment, its effect was lower compared to the CN3 treatment. On one hand, under N2 treatment, nitrogen levels were 20 % lower than normal nitrogen levels and did not significantly reduce yield, indicating that the N2 levels did not cause nitrogen deficiency, and the leaves could still maintain strong photosynthetic capacity, providing a assimilates source for yield formation. Du et al. (2022) also reported that reducing normal nitrogen by 0–40 % in China could maintain the rice yield. When the nitrogen level dropped to the N3 level (normal nitrogen decreases by 40 %), it led to a significant decrease in rice yield, indicating a lack of nitrogen supply. Nitrogen deficiency reduced the leaf area (Table 3), accelerated leaf senescence, reduced the photosynthetic rate (Fig. 3), and made the ability of flag leaves to supply sucrose to grains the main limiting factor for grain starch synthesis (Xiong, 2024; Guan et al., 2025), thereby increasing the demand for NSC accumulation in stems. The superior performance of the NCD enhanced leaf photosynthetic enzyme activity, stem and grain starch-related enzyme activity and gene expression, improved the grain sucrose supply capacity, promoted grain filling, and increased yield. However, while increase in yield could reach the level of N2, it did not surpass the levels of N2 and N1, indicating that the role of NCD could not replace the role of nitrogen. Under nitrogen deficit conditions, NCD played a role in alleviating yield decline, especially for indica rice, in which it had a more significant effect. This study provides a feasible solution for nitrogen reduction and stable production, which is of great significance for the green and sustainable development of rice. Further research is needed to determine the appropriate nitrogen reduction ratio and NCD application strategy for specific varieties.

Economic considerations are some of the main factors limiting the adoption of new technological methods. Specifically, net profits are the determining factor for the wide application of NCD in rice production via foliar spraying, therefore, we analyzed the costs and profits (Tables 4 and 5), two varieties had the same trend, using YY2640 as an example to illustrate (Table 4). The costs of seeds, pesticides, and phosphorus and potassium fertilizers, as well as the costs of mechanical and manual sowing, irrigation, weeding, and harvesting, were equal for all treatments. The difference in cost among treatments was the amount of nitrogen applied and the cost of the NCD. The difference in net profit was determined based on the total income from grain yield, and costs of fertilizer, NCD, and other costs. In both 2022 and 2023, for the N1 treatment, the fertilizer costs were the highest, but the highest revenue and net profits were obtained. The nitrogen input for the N2 and N3 treatments decreased by 20 % and 40 %, respectively, compared to the N1 treatment, and the yield reduced; therefore, the net profits decreased by USD 10 ha−1 and USD 604 ha−1 in 2022, USD 150 ha−1 and USD 1098 ha−1 in 2023, respectively. Treatments CN2 and CN3 resulted in an increase in yield due to the application of NCD, leading to an increase in total revenue and net profit. Compared to N1, the net profits decreased by USD 128 ha−1 and USD 234 ha−1 in 2022, USD 283 ha−1 and USD 696 ha−1 in 2023, respectively. Compared with N2, the net profits of CN2 were lower in both 2022 and 2023. However, compared with N3, net profits CN3 increased USD 370 ha−1 (19.7 %) and USD 402 ha−1 (25.8 %) in 2022 and 2023, reaching USD 2251 ha−1 and USD 1959 ha−1, respectively. In short, under nitrogen deficiency conditions, the foliar application of NCD can sharp increase the net profits of rice. The NCD used in this study were synthesized through a mild one-step electrochemical method without the need for any chemical reagents, requiring only pure water. This high-productivity method makes the NCD synthesis process both economical and environmentally friendly (Ming et al., 2012). Through continuous process optimization, the current production cost is as low as 0.1 USD L−1. Therefore, it was concluded that in low-yield areas, especially in developing countries where insufficient nitrogen fertilizer input leads to low yield levels, the foliar application of NCD in rice production is economically feasible.

In this study, under nitrogen deficiency (reductions in nitrogen input by 40 %), the foliar application of NCD before heading increased the accumulation of assimilates in the stem through increasing the activities of key enzymes involved in photosynthesis and starch synthesis. While at heading and during grain filling, the foliar application of NCD not only enhanced the activity of key enzymes involved in starch hydrolysis and sucrose synthesis in the stems, but also increased the activity of key enzymes involved in sucrose hydrolysis and starch synthesis in the grains. Meanwhile, it upregulated the expression of related genes involved in sucrose phloem loading in the stems and phloem unloading in the grains, all of which promoted the translocation of assimilates from the stems to the grains, thereby improving the grain filling percentage and grain yield of rice, and increase economic profits. In summary, the foliar spraying of NCD at jointing, heading, and 20 days after heading under nitrogen deficiency affected rice yield by regulating the physiological processes of carbon metabolism and translocation.
